content:
# Matthew Chapter 12 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 12 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 12:1 -- "At that time" vs. "In that season" vs. "Around that time"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "In that season" | Literal rendering of en ekeino to kairo |
| LOGOS | "At that time" | Standard formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Around that time" | Dynamic with added vagueness ("around") |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "At that time." The Greek kairos can mean "time" or "season," but "at that time" is the standard transitional phrase. PNEUMA's "around" adds temporal imprecision not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:2 -- "Look, your disciples are doing what is not lawful" vs. "Behold, the disciples of you do what is not lawful" vs. "Look! Your disciples are breaking the Sabbath law!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold, the disciples of you do what is not lawful to do on [a] Sabbath" | Literal with "behold" |
| LOGOS | "Look, your disciples are doing what is not lawful to do on the Sabbath" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Look! Your disciples are breaking the Sabbath law!" | Dynamic paraphrase condensing the accusation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "breaking the Sabbath law" is a valid summary but replaces the specific charge (doing what is "not lawful") with a broader accusation. The Pharisees' point is about specific prohibited actions, not Sabbath-breaking in general.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:4 -- "bread of the Presence" vs. "loaves of the presentation" vs. "sacred bread"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the loaves of the presentation" | Literal rendering of artous tes protheseos |
| LOGOS | "the bread of the Presence" | Traditional English biblical term |
| PNEUMA | "the sacred bread" | Dynamic simplification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "bread of the Presence." This is the established English rendering for the lechem hapanim / artoi tes protheseos, recognizable from OT translations. PNEUMA's "sacred bread" loses the specific cultic reference. EMET's "loaves of the presentation" is literal but unfamiliar.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:10 -- "withered hand" vs. "paralyzed hand"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a man having [a] withered hand" | Literal rendering of xeran (dried/withered) |
| LOGOS | "a man was there with a withered hand" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "A man with a paralyzed hand was there" | Dynamic substitution -- "paralyzed" for "withered" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "withered hand." The Greek xeros means "dried up" or "withered," not "paralyzed." These are different medical conditions. PNEUMA's substitution changes the nature of the affliction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's rendering is inaccurate to the Greek.

---

## 12:14 -- "conspired against him, how to destroy him" vs. "took counsel against him" vs. "began plotting how to kill him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "took counsel against him, how they might destroy him" | Literal with subjunctive preserved |
| LOGOS | "conspired against him, how to destroy him" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "walked out and began plotting how to kill him" | Dynamic with "kill" for "destroy" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "conspired." Both "destroy" (apollumi) and "kill" capture the intent, but apollumi is broader than "kill" -- it includes the sense of ruin and undoing. LOGOS's "conspired" captures the secretive, deliberate nature of sumboulion elabon.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:17 -- "Isaiah" vs. "Esaias"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Esaias the prophet" | Greek transliteration |
| LOGOS | "the prophet Isaiah" | Standard English name |
| PNEUMA | "the prophet Isaiah" | Standard English name |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the prophet Isaiah" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). English convention uses the anglicized "Isaiah."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 12:20 -- "smoldering wick" vs. "wick smoking" vs. "flickering candle"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a wick smoking he will not quench" | Literal with Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "a smoldering wick he will not quench" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "He won't snuff out a flickering candle" | Dynamic substitution -- "candle" for "wick" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "smoldering wick." The Greek linon tuphomenon refers to a flax wick that is smoking/smoldering -- a specific textile image. PNEUMA's "flickering candle" is vivid but introduces anachronistic imagery (candles were not the reference point) and changes "smoldering" (barely alive, smoking) to "flickering" (still burning), which alters the metaphor's force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The textile imagery of the wick is preserved.

---

## 12:22 -- "demon-oppressed" vs. "being demonized" vs. "demon-possessed"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a [man] being demonized" | Literal participle from daimonizomai |
| LOGOS | "a demon-oppressed man" | Formal with interpretive nuance |
| PNEUMA | "a man who was demon-possessed" | Traditional dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "demon-oppressed." The Greek daimonizomai is a broad term. "Possessed" (PNEUMA) implies total control, which may overstate the Greek. "Demonized" (EMET) is a transliteration. "Oppressed" captures the affliction without specifying the degree of demonic control.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:34 -- "brood of vipers" vs. "offspring of vipers" vs. "nest of snakes"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Offspring of vipers" | Literal gennema + echidna |
| LOGOS | "You brood of vipers!" | Traditional English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "You nest of snakes!" | Dynamic paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "brood of vipers." This is the recognized English rendering of gennemata echidnon, used since the KJV. PNEUMA's "nest of snakes" changes the metaphor -- "brood" refers to offspring (children of vipers), while "nest" refers to a dwelling place.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:36 -- "careless word" vs. "idle word" vs. "thoughtless word"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "every idle word" | Literal rendering of argon (inactive, useless) |
| LOGOS | "every careless word" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "every thoughtless word" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "careless." The Greek argon means "idle, inactive, useless" -- a word that accomplishes nothing good. "Careless" captures the negligence involved. "Idle" (EMET) is accurate but archaic. "Thoughtless" (PNEUMA) shifts the emphasis from the word's uselessness to the speaker's mental state.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:39 -- "adulterous" vs. "unfaithful"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "An evil and adulterous generation" | Literal moichalis |
| LOGOS | "An evil and adulterous generation" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "Only an evil, unfaithful generation" | Renders moichalis as "unfaithful" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "adulterous." The Greek moichalis means "adulterous" and carries the specific OT prophetic metaphor of Israel's idolatry as marital unfaithfulness to God. "Unfaithful" (PNEUMA) is a valid interpretation but loses the specific adultery/covenant metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 12:40 -- "great fish" vs. "sea-monster" vs. "great sea creature"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the belly of the sea-monster" | Renders ketos as "sea-monster" |
| LOGOS | "the belly of the great fish" | Renders ketos with Jonah narrative language |
| PNEUMA | "the belly of the great sea creature" | Neutral rendering of ketos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "great fish." The Greek ketos refers to a large sea creature (not specifically a whale). "Great fish" aligns with the Jonah narrative's own language and avoids both the mythological overtones of "sea-monster" (EMET) and the generic quality of "sea creature" (PNEUMA).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:45 -- "last state of that person" vs. "the last [things] of the man" vs. "The person ends up worse off"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the last [things] of the man that become worse than the first" | Literal with supplied elements |
| LOGOS | "the last state of that person is worse than the first" | Formal equivalent with "state" supplied |
| PNEUMA | "The person ends up worse off than before" | Dynamic simplification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "State" is a reasonable English supply for the neuter plural ta eschata. PNEUMA's rendering is accurate in meaning but loses the formulaic quality of the saying.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase vs. LOGOS/EMET formal/literal | 9 | LOGOS formal rendering preferred |
| EMET transliteration/archaic form vs. LOGOS standard | 3 | LOGOS standard English preferred |
| Word choice divergence (all three differ) | 6 | LOGOS chosen for balance |
| PNEUMA inaccuracy to Greek | 2 | LOGOS/EMET preferred (12:10 "withered," 12:34 "brood") |
| EMET/LOGOS agreement vs. PNEUMA | 2 | EMET/LOGOS rendering adopted |

### Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 12 continues the pattern from chapter 11: LOGOS provides the primary base for the consensus text, with EMET's literalism serving as a check on accuracy and PNEUMA's dynamic renderings offering readability insights. Two notable cases where PNEUMA's rendering was specifically rejected on accuracy grounds: "paralyzed hand" for "withered hand" (12:10) misidentifies the condition, and "nest of snakes" for "brood of vipers" (12:34) changes the metaphor from offspring to dwelling. PNEUMA's "flickering candle" for "smoldering wick" (12:20) is the most creatively divergent rendering, but it introduces anachronism and alters the metaphor's theological force. The Isaiah 42 Servant Song quotation (vv.18-21) was the most closely examined passage, with the wick/candle disagreement being the most consequential.

---

*Log compiled by SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
